planting.note: Plant acorns in fall immediately after collection or plant container-grown trees in spring in well-drained to moist, slightly acidic to neutral, deep, fertile to average, loamy or clay-loam soil in full sun; Hungarian oak tolerates a wide range of soils including heavy clays and is more drought-tolerant than most of the large European oaks.

directions: Plant acorns in fall or container-grown trees in spring in well-drained to moist, slightly acidic to neutral, deep, fertile to average, loamy or clay-loam soil in full sun; Hungarian oak is one of the most impressive and stately deciduous oaks of southeastern Europe — a massive, broadly spreading tree with exceptionally large, deeply pinnately lobed leaves (up to 10 inches long with large, rounded lobes) that are among the largest of any oak. Native to the open parkland forests, mixed deciduous forests, and scrubland of the Balkan Peninsula, Hungary, Italy, and southeastern Europe. Has become an important ornamental tree in western European and British gardens for its bold, large-leaved texture and massive, spreading form. More tolerant of heavy clay soils and moderate drought than most large-leaved European oaks. Relatively fast-growing for an oak. In the UK, the hybrid with sessile oak (Quercus x hispanica 'Lucombeana', the Lucombe oak) is a very ornamental semi-evergreen cultivar derived from Hungarian oak. An outstanding specimen tree for large landscape sites where maximum leaf impact and bold texture are desired.

planting.note: Plant seeds or container-grown trees in spring in well-drained to moist, slightly acidic, fertile to average, loamy or sandy soil in partial shade; ring-cupped oak grows naturally in the understory of moist mountain forests of Japan, China, and the Himalayas, making it more shade-tolerant than most oaks. Tolerates hot summers in the southeastern US.

directions: Plant seeds or container-grown trees in spring in well-drained to moist, slightly acidic, fertile to average, loamy or sandy soil in partial shade; ring-cupped oak is a beautiful East Asian evergreen oak widely distributed from Japan through China, Korea, and the Himalayas — an important component of warm-temperate and subtropical Asian forests. The attractive, evergreen, lanceolate to oblong leaves have characteristic waxy, blue-gray (glaucous) undersides that give the species its name. The acorn cups have distinctive concentric ring-shaped ridges (hence 'ring-cupped'). More shade-tolerant than most oaks — an unusual characteristic that makes it appropriate for partially shaded sites where most oaks would struggle. In Japanese gardens it is used as a refined evergreen specimen tree. Adaptable to the warm, humid southeastern US climate in zones 7–9. The glossy, dark-green, evergreen foliage provides year-round interest. An underused Asian evergreen oak for southern US gardens where holm oak might also be grown.

planting.note: Plant seeds or container-grown trees in spring in well-drained to moist, slightly acidic, fertile to average, loamy or sandy soil in partial to full shade; Japanese evergreen oak grows as a small understory tree in Japanese forests, tolerating considerable shade in youth. More cold-hardy than other Asian evergreen oaks, surviving brief dips to 10°F when well-established.

directions: Plant seeds or container-grown trees in spring in well-drained to moist, slightly acidic, fertile to average, loamy or sandy soil in partial to full shade; Japanese evergreen oak (akagashi in Japanese) is one of the most beautiful and important evergreen forest trees of Japan, Korea, and China — a relatively small (by oak standards) evergreen tree with glossy, dark green, simple, lanceolate to elliptic leaves and attractive pale-brown, smooth bark. In Japan it is an important forest tree of the warm-temperate evergreen broadleaf forest (laurel forest) zone along the Pacific coast. More shade-tolerant as a young tree than almost any other oak — it can grow as a woodland understory tree under a partial canopy where most oaks would be severely light-limited. The name 'akagashi' (red oak) in Japanese refers to the reddish new growth rather than any classification similarity to North American red oaks. An excellent evergreen shade tree for southeastern US gardens where a refined, glossy-leaved evergreen tree (unlike the course evergreen oaks) is desired. Rarely available but rewarding when found.

planting.note: Plant acorns in fall immediately after collection or plant container-grown trees in spring in well-drained, slightly alkaline to neutral, dry to average, rocky or loamy, lean to average soil in full sun; Portuguese oak is highly drought tolerant and adapted to the hot, dry, Mediterranean-type climate of Iberia. Very adaptable to poor, rocky, shallow soils in full sun.

directions: Plant acorns in fall or container-grown trees in spring in well-drained, slightly alkaline to neutral, dry to average, rocky or loamy, lean to average soil in full sun; Portuguese oak is the characteristic semi-evergreen oak of the western Mediterranean — a large, long-lived tree native to the Iberian Peninsula (Spain, Portugal) and northwestern Africa, growing in open forests, dry hillsides, and rocky slopes of the Mediterranean climate zone. Semi-evergreen in character — retaining most of its leaves through mild winters and shedding them in spring as new growth expands. The leaves are deeply lobed to entire, intermediate between deciduous and evergreen European oaks. Highly drought tolerant — one of the most drought-adapted of the European oaks, growing in areas with 12–20 inches of annual rainfall. Historically important in the dehesa (Spanish) and montado (Portuguese) agro-sylvo-pastoral systems — the open, grazed oak park-landscapes of Iberia that support unique biodiversity including Iberian lynx and black vultures. More heat and drought tolerant than English oak, suitable for Mediterranean-climate gardens of the US West Coast and similar warm, dry climates.

planting.note: Plant acorns in fall immediately after collection or plant container-grown trees in spring in well-drained to moist, slightly acidic to neutral, deep, fertile to average, loamy or sandy soil in full sun; Chinese cork oak develops a deep taproot and is highly drought tolerant once established. More cold-hardy than the European cork oak (Q. suber).

directions: Plant acorns in fall or container-grown trees in spring in well-drained to moist, slightly acidic to neutral, deep, fertile to average, loamy or sandy soil in full sun; Chinese cork oak is the Asian counterpart of the European cork oak (Quercus suber) — and in many ways a superior ornamental and more cold-hardy version of its famous cousin. Native across a huge range in Japan, Korea, and China, it develops a similarly distinctive thick, deeply furrowed, corky bark, but is deciduous (the European cork oak is evergreen) and considerably hardier to cold, surviving to zone 5 compared to zone 7-8 for cork oak. The chesnut-like toothed leaves are attractive and larger than the European cork oak's leaves. The acorns are covered almost entirely by the shaggy, bristly, elongated acorn cup scales — one of the most distinctive acorn features of any oak. An excellent ornamental for large gardens in zones 5–9 where the dramatic corky bark texture and bold silhouette are desired. More versatile climatically than cork oak but rarely grown in North America.
